content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting for Robust Code Generation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current prompting strategies for code generation in LLMs often fail to generalize to corner cases and complex specifications, resulting in brittle or incorrect code. Improving robustness and correctness in generated code remains a major challenge.",
  "motivation": "As LLM-powered code assistants are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, there is an urgent need to ensure their outputs handle edge cases and diverse requirements. Existing belief holds that prompting alone cannot substantially improve model robustness without explicit fine-tuning or auxiliary models. A novel prompting strategy could unlock latent capabilities of LLMs, bridging the gap between research accuracy and practical reliability.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Prompting', where for each code generation task, the prompt explicitly presents both the canonical specification and a constructed counterfactual scenario (i.e., a minimally changed input or requirement). The model is asked to generate code for both the original and counterfactuals, with a final synthesis step that integrates awareness of both cases. This approach conditions the model to reason over variations and anticipate edge cases, thus producing more robust and correct code. Prompt templates are automatically generated using input mutation techniques and static analysis to identify challenging counterfactuals.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS datasets, comparing against standard few-shot, chain-of-thought, and self-consistency prompting. Metrics include pass@k, functional correctness (via automated tests), and robustness to prompt perturbations. Ablation studies will assess the impact of counterfactual diversity and synthesis strategies."
}
